label: Feature Visualization
definition: A family of techniques that reveal what a neuron, channel, or learned feature responds to, either by optimizing an input to maximally activate it or by collecting the real inputs that activate it most strongly.

body.text: Feature visualization is the practice of making a network's internal units legible by showing what drives them. In its classic form, used heavily on vision models, you start from random noise and run optimization not on the weights but on the input pixels, ascending the activation of a single neuron or channel until the image becomes whatever that unit most wants to see. The synthetic images that emerge, edges and textures in early layers, then curves, eyes, and whole object motifs deeper in, give a direct picture of the abstraction each unit has learned. For language and other domains, the parallel and often more reliable technique is to scan a large corpus and collect the maximally activating examples, the real inputs on which the unit fires hardest.

The method matters because it turns an opaque parameter into a human-readable claim. Knowing that a particular channel activates for dog faces, or that a feature lights up on legal citations, is the raw material from which a mechanistic account is built. Feature visualization was one of the first tools to demonstrate that networks learn structured, nameable concepts rather than inscrutable statistics, and it remains the standard way to label the features that other methods surface. When a sparse autoencoder produces tens of thousands of candidate features, it is feature visualization, gathering each one's top activating inputs, that tells you what they actually mean.

Mechanically, the optimization variant is gradient ascent through the same computational graph used for training, but with the input as the free variable and a chosen activation as the objective. Left unconstrained it produces high-frequency adversarial noise that maximizes the unit without resembling anything natural, so practitioners add regularization and transformation robustness, jittering, rotating, and scaling the image each step, to coax out interpretable structure. The example-collection variant sidesteps this entirely by never leaving the data distribution, which is why it is the default for studying real models even though it can only show what the unit does on inputs you already have.

Feature visualization connects to the rest of interpretability as the descriptive half of a two-part method. It tells you what a unit represents but not whether that representation is causally used, which is why it is paired with interventions like ablation and activation steering that confirm a feature's role in the computation. It is also entangled with polysemanticity and superposition: when a single neuron's top activating inputs are an unrelated jumble, that is the visual signature of superposition, and it is precisely the symptom that pushes researchers from inspecting neurons toward the cleaner features recovered by a sparse autoencoder.
